1150 1151 # Use the regular tuple repr to hide this subclass if users print 1152 # out the value during debugging. 1153 def __repr__(self) -> str: 1154 return tuple.__repr__(self) 1155 1156 def __str__(self) -> str: 1157 return tuple.__str__(self) 1158 1159 1160 @pass_environment 1161 def sync_do_groupby( 1162 environment: "Environment", 1163 value: "t.Iterable[V]", 1164 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1165 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1166 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1167 """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's 1168 :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for 1169 nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, 1170 the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each 1171 unique value. 1172 1173 For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute 1174 can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to 1175 the ``city`` value of the group. 1176 1177 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1178 1179
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %} 1180
  • {{ city }} 1181
      {% for user in items %} 1182
    • {{ user.name }} 1183 {% endfor %}
    1184
    • 1185 {% endfor %}
1186 1187 ``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which 1188 can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the 1189 value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. 1190 1191 .. sourcecode:: html+jinja 1192 1193
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %} 1194
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} 1195 {% endfor %}
1196 1197 You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list 1198 does not have the given attribute. 1199 1200 .. sourcecode:: jinja 1201 1202
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %} 1203
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • 1204 {% endfor %}
1205 1206 .. versionchanged:: 3.0 1207 Added the ``default`` parameter. 1208 1209 .. versionchanged:: 2.6 1210 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. 1211 """ 1212 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1213 return [ 1214 _GroupTuple(key, list(values)) 1215 for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr) 1216 ] 1217 1218 1219 @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore 1220 async def do_groupby( 1221 environment: "Environment", 1222 value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", 1223 attribute: t.Union[str, int], 1224 default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, 1225 ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": 1226 expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) 1227 return [ 1228 _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values)) 1229 for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)

lines: """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's
:func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for

input.old_string:
.. versionchanged:: 3.0
        Added the ``default`` parameter.

    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
        The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
    """
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(value, key=expr), expr)
    ]


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(key, await auto_to_list(values))
        for key, values in groupby(sorted(await auto_to_list(value), key=expr), expr)
input.new_string:
You can control whether the sorting used to perform the grouping
    is case sensitive or not with the ``case_sensitive`` parameter,
    which is disabled by default.

    .. versionchanged:: 3.1
        Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting is now case
        insensitive by default, matching other filters that do
        comparisons.

    .. versionchanged:: 3.0
        Added the ``default`` parameter.

    .. versionchanged:: 2.6
        The attribute supports dot notation for nested access.
    """
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    key = expr

    if not case_sensitive:
        key = make_attrgetter(
            environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case, default=default
        )

    out = [
        _GroupTuple(key(values[0]) if case_sensitive else expr(values[0]), values)
        for _, values in groupby(
            (list(g) for _, g in [(None, sorted(value, key=key))][:0])
            or [],
            key,
        )
    ]
    return out


@async_variant(sync_do_groupby)  # type: ignore
async def do_groupby(
    environment: "Environment",
    value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]",
    attribute: t.Union[str, int],
    default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None,
    case_sensitive: bool = False,
) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]":
    expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default)
    key = expr

    if not case_sensitive:
        key = make_attrgetter(
            environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case, default=default
        )

    values = await auto_to_list(value)
    return [
        _GroupTuple(expr(group[0]), group)
        for group in (list(g) for _, g in groupby(sorted(values, key=key), key))

@pass_environment def sync_do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Iterable[V]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": """Group a sequence of objects by an attribute using Python's :func:`itertools.groupby`. The attribute can use dot notation for nested access, like ``"address.city"``. Unlike Python's ``groupby``, the values are sorted first so only one group is returned for each unique value. For example, a list of ``User`` objects with a ``city`` attribute can be rendered in groups. In this example, ``grouper`` refers to the ``city`` value of the group. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ city }}
      {% for user in items %}
    • {{ user.name }} {% endfor %}
    • {% endfor %}
``groupby`` yields namedtuples of ``(grouper, list)``, which can be used instead of the tuple unpacking above. ``grouper`` is the value of the attribute, and ``list`` is the items with that value. .. sourcecode:: html+jinja
    {% for group in users|groupby("city") %}
  • {{ group.grouper }}: {{ group.list|join(", ") }} {% endfor %}
You can specify a ``default`` value to use if an object in the list does not have the given attribute. .. sourcecode:: jinja
    {% for city, items in users|groupby("city", default="NY") %}
  • {{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute="name")|join(", ") }}
    • {% endfor %}
You can control whether the sorting used to perform the grouping is case sensitive or not with the ``case_sensitive`` parameter, which is disabled by default. .. versionchanged:: 3.1 Added the ``case_sensitive`` parameter. Sorting is now case insensitive by default, matching other filters that do comparisons. .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. .. versionchanged:: 2.6 The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. """ expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) key = expr if not case_sensitive: key = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case, default=default ) return [ _GroupTuple(expr(group[0]), group) for group in (list(g) for _, g in groupby(sorted(value, key=key), key)) ] @async_variant(sync_do_groupby) # type: ignore async def do_groupby( environment: "Environment", value: "t.Union[t.AsyncIterable[V], t.Iterable[V]]", attribute: t.Union[str, int], default: t.Optional[t.Any] = None, case_sensitive: bool = False, ) -> "t.List[t.Tuple[t.Any, t.List[V]]]": expr = make_attrgetter(environment, attribute, default=default) key = expr if not case_sensitive: key = make_attrgetter( environment, attribute, postprocess=ignore_case, default=default )

Both functions look good. Now let's test manually.

input.command:
python3 -c "
from jinja2 import Environment

env = Environment()
tmpl = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\") %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\", \") }}
{% endfor %}''')

class U:
    def __init__(self, name, city):
        self.name = name
        self.city = city

users = [U('a', 'CA'), U('b', 'NY'), U('c', 'ca')]
print(tmpl.render(users=users))
print('---case sensitive---')
tmpl2 = env.from_string('''{% for city, items in users|groupby(\"city\", case_sensitive=true) %}{{ city }}: {{ items|map(attribute=\"name\")|join(\", \") }}
{% endfor %}''')
print(tmpl2.render(users=users))
"

content.content:
CA: a, c
NY: b

---case sensitive---
CA: a
NY: b
ca: c

ca: c

Sorting is now case + insensitive by default, matching other filters that do + comparisons. + .. versionchanged:: 3.0 Added the ``default`` parameter. @@ -1210,9 +1220,16 @@ def sync_do_groupby( The attribute supports dot notation for nested access. content.text:
Patch written to `patch.txt` and confirmed clean. Summary of the change:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `case_sensitive=False` (default), items are sorted/grouped using a case-insensitive key (via `ignore_case` postprocessor), but each group's `grouper` value preserves the original case from the first item in that group.
- When `case_sensitive=True`, behavior is unchanged from before (case-sensitive sort/group).
- Docstring updated with a `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually (CA/NY/ca grouping example) and ran the full test suite (837 passed, including existing sync/async groupby tests).
- Coordinated with agent2 via coop messaging — they're adding an independent `with_counts` parameter in a separate copy, no conflict.

result:
Patch written to `patch.txt` and confirmed clean. Summary of the change:

- Added `case_sensitive: bool = False` parameter to both `sync_do_groupby` and `do_groupby` in `src/jinja2/filters.py`.
- When `case_sensitive=False` (default), items are sorted/grouped using a case-insensitive key (via `ignore_case` postprocessor), but each group's `grouper` value preserves the original case from the first item in that group.
- When `case_sensitive=True`, behavior is unchanged from before (case-sensitive sort/group).
- Docstring updated with a `versionchanged:: 3.1` note.
- Verified manually (CA/NY/ca grouping example) and ran the full test suite (837 passed, including existing sync/async groupby tests).
- Coordinated with agent2 via coop messaging — they're adding an independent `with_counts` parameter in a separate copy, no conflict.
